perturbation.original_text: BUYER HEREBY WAIVES ANY AND ALL ACTUAL OR POTENTIAL RIGHTS, CLAIMS, CAUSES OF ACTION OR DEMAND BUYER MIGHT HAVE REGARDING ANY FORM OF WARRANTY, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, OF ANY KIND OR TYPE, RELATING TO THE CONFIDENTIAL MATERIALS.
perturbation.changed_text: BUYER HEREBY WAIVES ANY AND ALL ACTUAL OR POTENTIAL RIGHTS, CLAIMS, CAUSES OF ACTION OR DEMAND BUYER MIGHT HAVE REGARDING ANY FORM OF WARRANTY, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, OF ANY KIND OR TYPE, RELATING TO THE CONFIDENTIAL MATERIALS, except for claims of intentional misrepresentation.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'except for claims of intentional misrepresentation', the contract implies all other warranty claims are waived. California law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract, which can be breached even without intentional misrepresentation. Waiving all other warranty claims is a legally risky position.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 1201 defines 'good faith' in the context of commercial transactions. By waiving nearly all warranties, the buyer could argue the seller isn't acting in good faith, especially if critical information affecting the property's value is withheld, even unintentionally. Omitting protection against breaches of good faith introduces legal risk.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 1201 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 1. GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person
perturbation.justification: The changed text waives all warranties except for intentional misrepresentation. The Law Explanation argues that this could be interpreted as the seller not acting in good faith, potentially violating California Commercial Code § 1201, especially if critical information is withheld unintentionally. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines key terms in the Commercial Code, including 'action,' 'aggrieved party,' and 'agreement.' By waiving nearly all warranties, the buyer's ability to pursue an 'action' as an 'aggrieved party' under the 'agreement' is significantly limited, potentially undermining the implied covenant of good faith, leading to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware while the property and parties are in California could be problematic. California has specific laws regarding real estate transactions, and applying Delaware law might circumvent certain consumer protections or disclosure requirements mandated by California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1646 states that a contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed. Since the property is in California, California law has a strong argument to apply, especially concerning real estate transactions and consumer protection. Choosing Delaware law may lead to disputes regarding enforceability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1646 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from California to Delaware. California Civil Code § 1646, as provided in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that a contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed, or where it is made if no place of performance is indicated. Given that the property and presumably the parties are in California, California law should govern the contract's interpretation, especially for real estate transactions and consumer protection. Changing the governing law to Delaware contradicts this principle and could potentially circumvent California laws, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer acknowledges that if this Agreement is breached, Owner could not be made whole by monetary damages. Accordingly, Owner, in addition to any other remedy to which it may be entitled by law or in equity, shall be entitled to an injunction to prevent breaches of this Agreement, and to an order compelling specific performance of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer acknowledges that if this Agreement is breached, Owner could not be made whole by monetary damages. Accordingly, Owner shall be entitled to an order compelling specific performance of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'in addition to any other remedy to which it may be entitled by law or in equity' and the reference to injunctions limits the Owner's remedies. Under California law, specifically regarding contracts, specific performance is not always granted and requires demonstrating the legal inadequacy of monetary damages. Limiting remedies creates legal risk if monetary damages would have been an option.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3384 states that specific performance may be compelled unless it is specifically barred by other sections. By limiting the owner's remedies solely to specific performance, the buyer risks challenges under California law which may allow for other remedies like monetary damages where appropriate, especially if specific performance is deemed unsuitable or impossible.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3384 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Specific Relief [3375 - 3415] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 3. Specific Performance of Obligations [[3384.] - 3395] ( Article 3 enacted 1872. ) [3384.] Section Thirty-three Hundred and Eighty-four. Except as otherwise provided in this Article, the specific performance of an obligation may be compelled. (Amended by Code Amendments 1873-74, Ch. 612.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract limits the owner's remedies to only specific performance, omitting the option for 'any other remedy to which it may be entitled by law or in equity,' including injunctions. California Civil Code § 3384 states that 'Except as otherwise provided in this Article, the specific performance of an obligation may be compelled.' The change could be problematic because specific performance is not always guaranteed, and by removing other potential remedies, the owner may not be adequately compensated if specific performance is not granted. The omission of other available remedies directly contradicts the intent of ensuring the owner has adequate recourse in case of a breach.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer shall at all times conduct its due diligence review, inspections and examinations in a manner so as to not cause damage, loss, cost or expense to Owner, the Property or the tenants of the Property and so as to not interfere with or disturb any tenant at the Property, and Buyer will indemnify, protect, defend, and hold Owner and the Property harmless from and against any such breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer shall at all times conduct its due diligence review, inspections and examinations, and Buyer will indemnify, protect, defend, and hold Owner and the Property harmless from and against any such breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'in a manner so as to not cause damage, loss, cost or expense to Owner, the Property or the tenants of the Property and so as to not interfere with or disturb any tenant at the Property' reduces the Buyer's obligation to conduct due diligence responsibly. California law implies a duty to avoid negligent or intentional harm to property. Removing this clause creates a legal risk if the Buyer's due diligence activities cause damage, even if unintentional.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1714 establishes the general principle that everyone is responsible for an injury caused to another by their want of ordinary care or skill in the management of their property or person. By removing the explicit requirement to conduct due diligence in a way that avoids harm, the contract becomes ambiguous and creates risk if the Buyer's actions result in damage to the property or tenants, potentially leading to liability issues despite the indemnification clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1714 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1714. (a) Everyone is responsible, not only for the result of his or her willful acts, but also for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill in the management of his or her property or person, except so far as the latter has, willfully or by want of ordinary care, brought the injury upon himself or herself. The design, distribution, or marketing of firearms and ammunition is not exempt from the duty to use ordinary care and skill that is required by this section. The extent of liability in these cases is defined by the Title on Compensatory Relief. (b) It is the intent of the Legislature to abrogate the holdings in cases such as Vesely v. Sager (1971) 5 Cal.3d 153, Bernhard v. Harrah’s Club (1976) 16 Cal.3d 313, and Coulter v. Superior Court (1978) 21 Cal.3d 144 and to reinstate the prior judicial interpretation of this section as it relates to proximate cause for injuries incurred as a result of furnishing alcoholic beverages to an intoxicated person, namely that the furnishing of alcoholic beverages is not the proximate cause of injuries result
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly requires the buyer to conduct due diligence in a way that avoids causing damage, loss, cost, or interference with tenants. The changed text removes this explicit requirement, potentially leading to a violation of California Civil Code § 1714, which states that everyone is responsible for injury caused to another by their want of ordinary care or skill in the management of their property. The omission weakens the protection against potential harm during the due diligence process, as the buyer is no longer explicitly obligated to avoid causing damage. This creates a contradiction as the contract now lacks a clear directive to prevent harm, increasing the risk of liability issues contrary to the general duty of care established by law.
